title: French toast
subtitle: Thick bread soaked in egg and milk, fried golden in butter
excerpt: Thick slices of white bread soaked in a mixture of egg, milk, and a little cinnamon, then fried in butter until golden and slightly puffed. Served with maple syrup. Simple and good, especially with day-old bread that has dried slightly and will absorb the custard without going to mush.
sourceNotes: Egg-soaked fried bread is documented across European cooking traditions for centuries; the French pain perdu (lost bread) and the English poor knights of Windsor are both versions. The American name French toast appears in print from the mid-nineteenth century. Fannie Farmer includes the preparation in The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544). Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) includes similar preparations under toast variations. A version also appears in the Roman cookbook attributed to Apicius (fourth and fifth century, the earliest surviving Latin cookbook).

content.text: French toast is thick bread soaked in a cinnamon egg custard and fried in butter until golden and slightly puffed. Serves two in about 15 minutes. Day-old bread works best: fresh bread has too much moisture and the centre goes heavy and wet.

The soaking time matters: 20 seconds per side for standard sandwich bread, up to 60 seconds for a thick slice of brioche or challah. The custard should reach the middle but not over-saturate the bread to the point where it falls apart when you pick it up. Fry in butter over medium heat; too hot and the outside browns before the centre warms through.
Make the custard and soak the bread
Crack the {{eggs}} into a wide, shallow bowl and whisk. Add the {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, and a pinch of salt. Whisk again to combine.
Submerge each slice of bread in the custard for 20 to 30 seconds per side, pressing gently to help absorption. Lift each slice and let the excess drip off for a few seconds before cooking.
Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the frying pan over medium heat. When the foam subsides, lay the soaked bread slices in the pan.

Cook for 2 to 3 minutes without touching until golden brown on the underside. Flip and cook for a further 2 minutes until the second side matches. The bread should feel slightly firm when pressed. If it is still very soft and wobbly in the centre, give it another minute per side on a lower heat.

items.fix: Limit soaking to 30 seconds per side; allow excess custard to drip off before frying
items.cause: Bread soaked too long, or too much custard absorbed
items.symptom: French toast is soggy in the centre

items.fix: Cook on medium heat; lower the heat slightly after flipping if the crust is browning too fast
items.symptom: Outer crust is dark but centre is raw

items.fix: Use day-old bread; or toast the slices lightly before soaking to firm the structure
items.cause: Bread too fresh and soft, or soaked too long
items.symptom: Bread falls apart in the custard

items.fix: Taste the custard before soaking. It should taste clearly of cinnamon. Add more if it does not.
items.cause: Too little cinnamon or sugar in the mix

content.text: French toast is one of the oldest egg-and-bread dishes in the world. It appears under dozens of names across dozens of cuisines. In France it is pain perdu (lost bread), a name that names its first purpose: reviving stale bread that would otherwise be thrown out. The American version is sweeter and thicker. It is served as a morning dish, not a dessert or a savoury course. That is the version most people now know.
